CURRENT AFFAIRS,
. We read with some amusement the political harangue
of Dr. Bunbury, Bishop of the Church of England’s
Faithful Garrison in Limerick. It is a long time now
since we had occasion to refer to Dr. Bunbury, and his
speech in some respects significantly enough struck us
for its quality of comparative mildness. Dr. Bunbury
first referred to finance. Oh, that money, money !
Evidently some of his lordship’s flock have the
< gconomic sense ’’ s0 strongly developed that they will
give nothing to the Auxiliary Fund. ¢ On the other
hand,” said he, ‘“‘not a few with larger means. had
given nothing up to now.” Perhaps, the attacks of the
“<“gaved ”” on the ¢ extravagance "’ of ‘“Idolatrous’’
Church-building is percolating back, as it were, to the
- hearts of some of the ‘“saved,” and their ”ec.:onomic

sense *’ may have become shocked at the extravagance
of the Auxiliary Fund! A sort of poetic retribution,
eh!

Dr. Bunbury, we are glad to observe, does not like
sectarian bitterness—he is probably coming round to
our view. Our almost single-handed fight for tolerance
would indeed be largely successful if it took the scales
from the eyes of a large number of the “saved,” and
induced them to resolve to cease being bigots in future.
“ But what prospect,”’ asked Dr. Bunbury, ‘‘could
there be of peace and prosperity whilst sectarian
animosity existed between Protestants and Roman
Catholics$’ That is a pertinent question. We have
been three years agitating for Justice, Tolerance, and
Fair Play in the staffing of the Great Northern Rail-
way Co., and notwithstanding all the agitation, and the
blocking work done by the Irish Party in the British
Parliament, the Great Northern held out to the last
gasp and so far have only given in in a bogus sort of
way with their arrangement for giving marks for
“manner.””  “In no country in the world was it
(sectarian animosity) so rife as in ours, both in the
North and in the South—in the former, perhaps, more
than in the latter.”” Quite so, but the ‘ perhaps’’ is
altogether unnecessary. In those parts of the North
where England’s Faithful Garrison are in the majority
their sectarian animosity is naturally more noticeable
than in the South where they are in a minority. Be-
sides, in the South, they have to play the game more
quietly. Dr. Bunbury further said: —‘ But- it was
pleasant to hear from reliable sources that signs of a
more tolerant spirit were apparent in the North, and
he hoped they were not wrong in recognising a similar
spirit in the South.” We sincerely hope that this is
the case. However, the conduct of the Great Northern
Railway directors would not lead one to be unduly
optimistic of the growth of tolerance amongst his lord-
ship’s party in the North-East corner. .

Dr. Bunbury asked another interesting question:—
 Was it hopeless to expect Protestants and Roman
Catholics to sink as much as possible their religious
differences, and try to live in charity with each other ?”’
As for the Catholics they have always been charitable
and tolerant—in fact, too many of them have stepped
beyond the limits and are tame.”” As for the Pro-
testants we welcome any signs of a growth of the virtue
of charity amongst them, but we regret to say we don’t,
see very many signs. Why, it was only the other day
that an oficial document of the General Assembly of
the Presbyterians in Ireland said of the Catholic Church
that ¢ The Papacy has, instead of Christ, given to the
Trish people a cruel and rapacious church'’; instead of
the Holy Spirit it has provided a heartless and selfish
priest ; instead of the word of God it has substituted
sensuous sacraments and ceremonies, which do not call
out faith, but only benumb the soul with superstition.”’
There is not much evidence in that Presbyterian circular

The serious nature of the “bona fide” nuisance at
such places as Booterstown was emphasised by a recent
Prosecution of a bogus ““bona’’ who was found on the
Premises of a bungery owner by the name of Johu
Downey. According to evidence there wers 40 persons
in the bungery, and over 100 in the yard attached.
Ov‘er 140 “bonas”’ on the premises of one Booterstown
drink shop on a Sunday! Downey, ‘who runs this
*{h’lnk _shop, and who was fined £5 in 1903 for supply-
ing drink to a drunken man, was fined £2 on the head
of this bogus “bona.” It is not without a smile that
one reads that the name of the man on guard at the
door is John Plant, and that the policeman who bagged
the bogus “bona’’ is by name Sergeant Beery! Send
a Beery to catch a “bona!’”’

A great outburst of Orange raimeis took place onc
day near Bushmills, where, appropriately enough,
whiskey is made. The resolution proposed by Captain
Craig, M.P., bore out one of our contentions—namély,
that “ The King ”’ is a party cry in Ireland. The reso-
lution concluded with these words—* And ‘to reiterate
our watchword, “God Save the King.””’ Here we ‘have
it officially announced ‘that the cry “God Save the
King ”’ is the watchword of the sectarian and anti-Irish
Orange Society.

Said the gallant Craig, M.P.—“ It would do him (Sir
A. MacDonnell) good to be at Dunseverick that day,
and to study the countenances of the sturdy race
arcund.”” A sour, a very sour, study we should sug-
gest!

Mr. Moore, K.C., D.L., and ex-M.P., proposed a
resolution at this meeting which declared that ‘they
would “bitterly oppose,”’ and so forth. We have no
doubt about the bitter part of it; how could a Sourface
escape being bitter?

That portion of England’s Faithful Garrison in Tre-
land who were concerned in the erecting of a memorial
at Killarney to the various Tommies who died on active
British service from 1881 to 1902 succeeded in getting
over an English Catholic Bishop to make a speech on
the occasion. The English Catholic Bishop would be
better advised if he refrained from mixing up with
England’s Faithful Garrison in this country.  The
English Catholic Bishop, Dr. Brindle, .said that “he
was in the presence of an audience essentially and in-
tensely—he was glad to use the word—Irish, and he
was just as much an Englishman.”” He was in pre-
sence of nothing of the sort; he was in the presence of
a pack of Sourfaces and imitation Englishmen. Dr.
Brindle said: “They (the Tommies) gave their lives
willingly in the cause of duty.”” We beg ito contradict
his lordship. They did nothing of the sort, they gave
them for a shilling a day. Of course the Irish T'umes
has a leading article on it, and the libeller of Canon
MecInerney pours honey and treacle on the English

© . Catholic Bishop who came over here at ‘the invitation

of the Emgland’s Faithful Garrison’s clique to this
. ascendancy function. The [rish 7'imes says: ‘ Morally
and socially the soldier of the present time bears no

We have already referred to some questions put by
Mr. Robert Russell, M.A., F.T.C.D., in the Geometry
papers at this year’s Intermediate. There are other
curious things concerning the Intermediafo besides
blundering questions in Geometry papers. If three
papers—moderate, difficult, and extremely difficult—
be set as tests of excellence in any branch of study a
pupil who wins 26 marks above “ honours’’ on the most
difficult of the three should, one would think, at least
be granted a “pass’’ in that subject. However, a
strango thing happened in connection with a pupil of
a convent school. A competitor in the Senior Grade
won an “ honour’’ certificate in French, but was marked
as having “failed ”’ in the subject! The convent autho-
rities sent for their pupil’s marks, and found that she
won higher marks for French than for any of her
“ honours ’’ subjects, but she was one per cent. short
of a pass on the “Honours’’ paper though she had
won 26 marks beyond 50 per cent. on the difficult test
special paper. She won’t, however, be granted the
word “pass’’ in her French on her certificate. How
is this? Can.an Intermediate “pass’’ be looked on as
a guarantee of knowledge of any subject when a pupil
who wins 226 marks out of 400 is marked as a failure,
especially when it is declared that the “special *’ paper
is to be of a more difficult and testing nature? Where
is the .test when “failed ” is written after “ Honours’’
on it:?

Who is E. Mitchell, Draper, of Ennis, in the
Banner County? Of what class are his customers?
Anyway he wants an energetic salesman, and the ener-
getic salesman must be “Saved.” We wonder does
ho .insist upon his customers being “saved,”’ and does
he advertise in .the local papers that mere “ Idolators’’
are not wanted as customers in his shop? From the
DBelfast News Letter of Friday last we take the follow-
ing:—“ Drapery.—Energetic Salesman Wanted for
General Trade; Protestant.  Apply, with references,
stating lowest salary, to E. Mitchell, Ennis, Co. Clare.”

Messrs. Valentin and Co., of Hamburg, must have
picked out the name of the LEADER from a Press Direc-
tory; certainly they cannot know anything of the
paper. Valentin and Co. exploit a Hamburg lottery,
and Hamburg lotteries are advertised in many of our
“truly national’’ provincial papers accompanied by
editorial puffs. Perhaps Valentin and Co. think all
papers in Ireland are on the level of those in which
they advertise. They sent us their advertisement, and
if they sont us a hundred pounds sterling for one inser-
tion we would not insert it. Heaven knows there is
enough gambling and betting in Ireland without
encouraging green people to join Hamburg lotteries.

However, we were glad that Valentin and Co., of
Hamburg, proffered us their advertisement, for we
learned the price which they pay. The advertisement
is set out in two columns, but we suppose they would
not object to it appearing in one column as Hamburg
lotteries appear in some of our provincial contem-
poraries.  Set out in one column, it would take up
about three-quarters of a Kreeman's Journal column,
and Valentin and Co. say— We are disposed to pay

One day last month Miss Eileen Murphy, daughter
of Dr. J. J. Murphy, of Harcourt Street, Dublin, re-
ceived the white veil in the historic convent of the Irish
Benedictine Nuns at Ypres, where the flag of the Irish
Brigade and flags of Ramillies were deposited. In those
Irish-Treland days one naturally foels that if the “ Wild
Geese’’ had fought in Ireland instead of at Ramillies
and other places, it would have been better for the
country, and likewise many of us regret the emigration
of nuns as we do of other people. However, though
that be so, those of our readers who know Dr. J. J.
I Murphy personally, or who know him by repute, will
join in our congratulations to him on the reception of
his daughter at a convent that has such historic asso-
ciations that link it with Ireland.

A lady complains to us of the backward state of Ros-
crea, in Gallant Tip., in connection with the Industrial
Revival. She writes:—“I think it is about time to
draw your attention to Roscrea—it is not going in much
for Irish goods—in fact you can get hardly anything
Trish.”” She also says:— I went into a shop in one of
the squares of Roscrea for flour and oatmeal. I asked
what meal was that? I was told, the best American.
I said, I won’t have any but Irish. (They hadn’t it in
s the shop). The flour was Budapest, the best, of course.
| Now I ask you, is it not too bad ¥’ We certainly agree
that it is too bad. Gallant Tip. appears to be a poor
i place in theso days. Are they all English now in Gal-
lant Tip.? Cahir, of the Tommies, we can understand,
but we are surprised at Roscrea.

The June issue of Freddy Trench’s Budget, self-styled ;
“‘Grievances from Ireland,” compiled by that well-known 3
genius, Frederick Oliver Trench, better known now
to his own household and employees as Lord Ash-
town, has been sent to us. It is curious to observe how
men of genius like Freddy Trench amuse themselves in
their spare moments. Precious time that was intended
for the advancement of mankind is taken up making
clippings for the Big Brother. ~And Freddy is a Privy
Councillor, too! Freddy, though a real live “ Lord,”
is sending round the hat for his Budget; perhaps he
thinks the servant girls of Manchester and of the slums
of London will send in subscriptions for the persecuted
landlords and the half-starving Ascendancy people!
Lord Freddy says —

The salary of a competent editor, who must practically devote
the whole of his time to the work, has to be provided. The cost
of printing and binding “Griovances from Ireland” forms a heavy
item of expense every year; nor ocan the work of collecting reliable:
information from every corner of Ireland every month be put
down at less than £90 per annum. The postage and statjonery
items are always very serious. Clerks have to bo employed for
directing and folding for the posb; a large private correspondence
must be kept up; and to all theso items must be added a fair
proportion for rent of offices.

‘Who could resist that eloquent plea? We are sure
that Charles E. Martin would send on a fine, thumping
subscription  to the laudable work only that Lord
Freddy works it under the auspices of “ The Imperial b
Protestant Federation !’ R

We see that the June number of Freddy Trench’s
Budget devotes a few pages to the Catholic Defence
Society. “The LEADER, a Nationalist paper published
in Dublin,” comes under the rod of Freddy Trench.
I'reddy Trench’s Budget says:—‘“ In a leading article,
under the title of “The Catholic Defence Society,”’ which
appeared in its (the LEADER’S) issue of May 12th, 1906,
it holds.up the new society to admiration, on the ground
that none but Roman Catholics are allowed into its
ranks.”” Fancy that, now, Freddy! Freddy Trench’s
Budget is much concerned also with an article we wrote,

The Capuchin College at Rochestown, Co. Cork, is a
-great educational centre of Irish Ireland. Speaking at
the annual meeting of the Cork Gaelic League, Father
Augustine, of Rochestown College, said :—

We' have a branch of the Gaelio League in the Colloge, and
last year we won the two silver medals of the Fleming Companion-
ship in tho Hall of Readers and the Hall of Writers, tho first,
second, and fourth places in tho Intermediate, first. placo also in
Junior Grade, thereby winning the much-coveted bronze modal—
the only one given in that grade—and a special prize for excollence
in Irish composition. This year we won the silvor medal in the

. Hall of Writers, and the gold medal in the Lowor Court of
Romance. In the Intermodiate we won first and second places in
the Junior Grade, with the bronze medal and a special prizo for
composition.  We won first place also in the Middle Grade with
the medal, and a special prize for excellence in Irish composition,
and please noto this important fact—oach of the boys that won
these honours and medals also won an exhibition, which plainly
shows that we can teach other things than Irish in Rochestown,
and that our devotion to Irish not only does not hamper the boys,
but rather opens out their minds, and makes them bettor able to
grasp the other subjects of the Intermediate programmo,

The annual concerts in aid of the Blind Asylum,
Merrion, are deservedly among the more popular musi-
cal fixtures in Dublin. They take place this year on
Wednesday and Thursday evenings next, and will, as
usual, be held in the Rotunda. Those who have
attended previous concerts will need little inducement
to go again, as, apart from the sympathy and support
which the sad circumstances of the performers naturally
arouse, the standard of instrumental and vocal skill dis-
played at past concerts should be sufficient incentive to
lovers of music.

‘Wherever Father Mathew Maguire, P.P., goes, in
that place up springs a branch of the Gaelic League.
In the ordinary course of things he has officiated in
various districts, and he plants at each remove a pros-
perous branch of the Gaelic League. ~‘When he was
C.C. of Dromore, that little town won thetitle of Irish
Dromore. Now he is Parish Priest of Kilskeery, and
on Sunday week what we may call the inevitable branch
of the Gaelic League was formally launched. Evyening
Irish classes, under the tuition of Mr. P. Waldron, of
Ballyhaunis, are already in operation at Golan, Tril-
lick, Roscor and Drumharvey Schools. Other classes
will be opened shortly. The formal opening of the
Gaelic League at Kilskeery was a very successful and
important function. Besides an address from Father
Mathew Maguire himself, Mr. Thomas Concannon and
Father T. A. Finlay, S.J., delivered speeches.

Next Sunday the great Temperance Procession takes
place through the streets of Dublin. Walking in a pro-
cession is one way of demonstrating in the cause of
Temperance ; another way, and one which will not in-
terfere with the former, is no¢ to walk inté a bungery
either before the procession commences or when it is
over. Indeed, in honour of the day, we would not
thank the publicans very much if they shut up their
drink shops on Sunday; but, of course, they won’t do
anything of the sort. The spongers on St. Patrick are
only amenable—green patriots as they are—to the pres-
sure of British law. We were glad to see that Mr. P.
J. O’Neill, in the course of his address at the Father
Mathew Hall, referred to the scandalous action of some
of the members of the Irish Parliamentary Party in
opposing the Irish Liquor Bill recently before the
British Parliament.

Next Sunday’s procession, which we are sure will be
an imposing demonstration, should give a great impetus
to the Temperance movement. We are confiden that if
we had some sort of working Home Rule that the force
of Irish public opinion would soon assert itself in the
laws governing the distribution of intoxicating liquor in
this country, and Mr. Bung would find he could not
confuse the issue by putting on a greon coat, and strik-
ing an attitude as a toy rebel! However, as we have
not Home Rule, we can, at least, refuse to get drunk
in spite of Dublin Castlo Government. On Sunday it
would be a great thing if the Irish working man in
Dublin left the public-houses and their patriotic owners
to enjoy the exclusive patronage of Tommy Atkins.

T is to be hoped that the discussion that is taking
place in these columns on the: question of an His-
torical Society may not end in the mere ventilation of
different views, but may be followed by some effective
action. No one, I should think, who knows the country
will have any doubt that there is grave need for the
dissemination of historical knowledge amongst our
people. I do not believe, however, that it is a matter
of very pressing concern whether the bulk of such
knowledge be scientific or not.  Under the influences
at present at work in Ireland, there will surely be found
amongst our intellectual people a sufficient number
who will have both the inclination and the aptitude to
attack the study. of Irish history in.the modern spirit,
and with modern methods. Such persons may well be
left to take care of themselves. The bulk of the people,
however, would require to be seen to, and that
promptly. Even if we cannot hope to make them criti-
cal historians in the mass, we can surely still do some-
thing in the way of informing their intelligences, and
deepening and widening the affections of their hearts.
The latter of these objects—in spite of what some people
would appear to think—is one that counts for some-
thing. and cannot, with safety, be overlooked. Every-
one knows- how difficult it is to make an impression on
a- hostile audience, even when one has the best of
cases, and. presents it with the mest solid arguments;
and an indifferent, or unsympathetic, audience is
nearly as bad. If Balldearg O’Donnell were to come
to-morrow, or Manannan MacLir and his men, °
awakening from their long sleep, to emerge from under
Royal Greenan; or-some other heaven-sent leader, or
leaders, to appear in our midst, one; or all of them,
might find it difficult to free the country, if the country
itself had no anxiety to be free. Now, has the country
actually any such anxiety to be free? There was a
time, no doubt, when such a feeling was pretty general.
That was when things were black for the individual as
well as for the nation as a nation—when the bulk of
the people, without means of escape, were subjected to
a tyranny that even at this lapse of time the mind can-
not contemplate without horror. In the Ireland of
to-day, that is changed: The individual as such enjoys
a very considerable degree of liberty,.and the 'sober man
who has a'decent farm, and works it, or a fortior: a
prosperous public-house, is usually a person far from
miserable. A bigtlot' of people jog along’in. Ireland
with comparative comfort, and with greater: ease’ than
perhaps anywhere else in.the world. There is-difficulty,. '
no doubt, when the family grows up, if its- members
are Papists: But even if there'is not much.opening: at
home' for the young boys: and-girls, cannot they: always
go to England, or America, where, in' many respects,
they will be as much at home as if they went to the:
